refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable end-to-end teleoperation communication with latency that permits safe remote operation by the tele-operator.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting teleoperation capability, and (2) ensuring latency is compatible with safety. The refined version separates the functional intent (enable teleoperation communication) from the performance constraint (latency compatible with safety). The acceptance criterion (p95 ≤ 150 ms) is a quantified DP/IM-level specification, not part of the FR itself. The FR now resides purely in the problem domain without prescribing technology (e.g., 'communication latency' rather than 'network protocol' or '5G').
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency (sensor input → tele-operator display + tele-operator command → equipment actuation) under agreed network conditions. Confirm p95 latency ≤ 150 ms. Validate through tele-operator acceptance testing and safety case review that measured latency permits safe operation per worksite operational policies.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The NGHE solution shall transmit equipment state (sensor data, video, telemetry) to the tele-operator with minimal delay.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The NGHE solution shall transmit tele-operator commands (control inputs) to the equipment with minimal delay.
FR-NGHE-008.3: The NGHE solution shall provide tele-operator feedback (visual, auditory, haptic) sufficient to enable safe remote operation.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement a deterministic, low-latency communication pipeline with end-to-end latency budgeting across acquisition, transmission, processing, and actuation stages, with jitter control and priority queuing to meet p95 ≤ 150 ms under specified network conditions.
DP2: Establish a dual-path communication architecture (primary + failover) with automatic switchover logic and latency monitoring to ensure safe operation during network degradation.
DP3: Decompose teleoperation into independent functional channels (command, feedback, safety) with separate latency budgets and isolation mechanisms to prevent cross-channel interference.

DP4: Employ adaptive bitrate and compression strategies with latency-aware codec selection to maintain p95 ≤ 150 ms across variable network conditions without sacrificing safety-critical feedback fidelity.
DP5: Implement a time-synchronized, event-driven architecture with predictable scheduling and bounded processing queues to eliminate non-deterministic delays in the communication loop.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP3: Decompose teleoperation into independent functional channels (command, feedback, safety) with separate latency budgets and isolation mechanisms to prevent cross-channel interference, combined with DP1's deterministic pipeline and latency budgeting discipline applied per channel.

potential_sources: Shared network infrastructure (single link) creates implicit coupling between channels; mitigated by QoS/priority tagging and separate logical flows.
Feedback latency influences operator perception and command timing; managed by explicit latency budget allocation and operator training per worksite safety plan.
Failover logic (DP2) introduces state synchronization complexity; addressed by stateless command design and idempotent safety actions.
Codec/compression selection (DP4) may conflict with determinism; resolved by pre-selecting fixed codecs and testing under worst-case network conditions.
coupling_check.notes: DP3 + DP1 combination achieves low coupling by isolating functional concerns and applying deterministic budgeting per channel. Residual coupling is managed through explicit QoS policies and operational constraints (worksite safety plan). No architectural deadlock or circular dependency detected.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Network conditions are stable and predictable within the agreed envelope (latency, jitter, packet loss); RISK: Unmodeled network anomalies (e.g., congestion spikes, asymmetric paths) may violate p95 guarantee. MITIGATION: Continuous latency monitoring and fallback to reduced-capability mode.

ASSUMPTION: Tele-operator cognitive load and reaction time are compatible with 150 ms round-trip latency; RISK: Operator fatigue or unfamiliarity may degrade safety. MITIGATION: Operator training, worksite safety plan enforcement, and task-specific latency budgets.

ASSUMPTION: Safety-critical feedback (e.g., collision detection, emergency stop) can be isolated from non-critical telemetry; RISK: Incomplete feedback may mask hazards. MITIGATION: Redundant safety sensors and independent safety channel with stricter latency budget (e.g., p95 ≤ 100 ms).

ASSUMPTION: Deterministic scheduling and bounded queues are achievable in the chosen execution environment; RISK: OS/runtime jitter may violate latency bounds. MITIGATION: Real-time OS or containerized isolation with latency testing under load.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan and operational policies are enforced consistently; RISK: Operator deviation or policy gaps may negate latency benefits. MITIGATION: Automated policy enforcement (e.g., geofencing, speed limits) and audit logging.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly separate safety-critical feedback (e.g., 'The NGHE solution shall isolate and prioritize safety-critical feedback channels with p95 ≤ 100 ms') from non-critical telemetry (p95 ≤ 150 ms). Add acceptance criteria for failover latency and operator reaction time validation. Clarify 'agreed network conditions' with quantified bounds (e.g., RTT, jitter, packet loss %). Introduce a secondary FR for latency monitoring and graceful degradation to ensure the system can detect and respond to latency violations without operator intervention.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates all teleoperation-specific functional decomposition: command channel processing, feedback channel processing, safety channel isolation, per-channel latency budgeting, deterministic pipeline orchestration, and cross-channel interference prevention mechanisms.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands and mission directives; routed through command channel with dedicated latency budget

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Outputs vehicle state feedback and sensor data; routed through feedback channel with independent latency budget

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional safety channel: receives safety constraints/alerts from Safety Services; outputs safety-critical decisions and override signals

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Underlying communication substrate; Teleoperation manages channel multiplexing, prioritization, and latency isolation across all three functional channels

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment. DP3 is fundamentally a teleoperation-domain concern: channel decomposition, latency budgeting per channel, and interference prevention are core to remote operation architecture. The three channels (command, feedback, safety) and their isolation mechanisms naturally reside within this boundary. Minor diffusion: safety channel must interface with Safety Services (SafetyAlert), and network multiplexing depends on Network Link substrate, but these are well-defined external contracts.

Poor containment. Safety Services owns safety policy and constraint enforcement, not teleoperation channel architecture. DP3's command and feedback channels have no logical home in Safety Services. The safety channel component would be contained, but command and feedback channel logic would diffuse into Teleoperation anyway, violating cohesion. This creates split ownership and weak encapsulation.

Very poor containment. Vehicle owns actuators, sensors, and vehicle-side state management. DP3 is about teleoperation protocol and channel isolation on the operator/communication side, not vehicle internals. Placing channel decomposition and latency budgeting in Vehicle creates severe diffusion: command processing, feedback aggregation, and safety isolation all belong upstream in the teleoperation layer. This violates separation of concerns.

Poor containment. NGHE Solution is a top-level system container, not a functional module. DP3 is a specific architectural pattern for teleoperation; placing it at the solution level dilutes ownership and makes the pattern invisible to subsystem design. Diffusion is high because the pattern's details would scatter across multiple subsystems without a clear owner.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP3 (channel decomposition, per-channel latency budgeting, and cross-channel isolation) is a teleoperation-specific architectural pattern. The Teleoperation module is the natural owner because: (1) it is responsible for managing the operator-to-vehicle communication protocol and real-time constraints; (2) the three functional channels (command, feedback, safety) are abstractions of the teleoperation domain, not vehicle or safety domain concerns; (3) latency budgeting per channel is a teleoperation scheduling and resource allocation problem; (4) interference prevention mechanisms (e.g., priority queuing, buffer isolation, timeout enforcement) are teleoperation middleware responsibilities. External dependencies (SafetyAlert, Network Link, Tasking, Telemetry) are well-defined interfaces that do not require diffusion of core DP3 logic.
containment_risks: Safety channel isolation may require tight coupling with Safety Services (SafetyAlert interface). Risk: if safety policy changes, teleoperation channel architecture must adapt. Mitigation: define SafetyAlert as a contract-based interface with clear semantics; safety policy logic remains in Safety Services, teleoperation only enforces channel-level constraints.

Network Link substrate may impose constraints on channel multiplexing (e.g., bandwidth limits, jitter). Risk: latency budgets per channel may be infeasible if Network Link cannot guarantee isolation. Mitigation: establish Network Link SLA upfront; if SLA cannot be met, revise DP3 to include adaptive latency budgeting or fallback modes.

Deterministic pipeline (DP1) integration requires coordination with Vehicle-side processing. Risk: if Vehicle does not implement corresponding deterministic pipeline, end-to-end latency guarantees fail. Mitigation: DP1 and DP3 must be co-designed; Vehicle module must implement matching deterministic pipeline for command execution and feedback generation.

Cross-channel interference prevention (e.g., priority inversion, starvation) is complex in real-time systems. Risk: implementation may leak into Network Link or Vehicle modules if not carefully abstracted. Mitigation: define clear IM boundaries for each channel's queue, scheduler, and timeout handler; encapsulate within Teleoperation.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: No revision to DP3 is recommended at this stage. DP3 is well-scoped to the Teleoperation module and does not require implementation-specific details. However, recommend explicit co-design of DP1 (deterministic pipeline) and DP3 (channel decomposition) to ensure end-to-end latency guarantees. Additionally, clarify the contract between Teleoperation and Safety Services: define which safety constraints are enforced at the channel level (Teleoperation responsibility) versus which are enforced at the policy level (Safety Services responsibility). This will prevent diffusion and ensure clean interfaces.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR specifies a quantitative acceptance criterion (p95 latency ≤ 150 ms) that is directly measurable through end-to-end teleoperation testing under agreed network conditions. The Teleoperation IM boundary encapsulates all three functional channels (command, feedback, safety) with independent latency budgets, enabling per-channel instrumentation and verification. Latency can be measured at external interfaces (Tasking in, Telemetry out, SafetyAlert bidirectional) and correlated to channel-level metrics within the module. The deterministic pipeline (DP1) provides a structured basis for latency analysis.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: High. The FR requirement maps unambiguously to the Teleoperation module. The three functional channels (command, feedback, safety) with separate latency budgets and isolation mechanisms are explicitly scoped within the IM boundary. External dependencies (Network Link substrate, Safety Services policy, Vehicle-side deterministic pipeline) are identified as contracts, not diffused responsibilities. The SafetyAlert interface clearly delineates which safety constraints are enforced at the channel level (Teleoperation) versus policy level (Safety Services). Tasking and Telemetry interfaces establish clear data flow boundaries.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP3 directly addresses the FR requirement. The decomposition into independent functional channels (command, feedback, safety) with separate latency budgets and isolation mechanisms is a sound architectural approach to enabling safe remote operation under latency constraints. The combination of DP3 with DP1's deterministic pipeline and latency budgeting discipline provides a coherent solution path. The DP is expressed at the appropriate level of abstraction (architectural pattern) without prescribing implementation details. No logical gaps or contradictions detected between FR and DP.

diffusion_risks: Safety channel isolation (SafetyAlert interface) creates a coupling point with Safety Services. Risk: if safety policy semantics change (e.g., new constraint types, alert formats), the Teleoperation module's channel-level enforcement logic may require adaptation. Mitigation: establish SafetyAlert as a versioned contract interface with explicit schema and semantics; safety policy logic remains owned by Safety Services; Teleoperation enforces only channel-level constraints (priority, timeout, buffer isolation) independent of policy content.

Network Link substrate multiplexing and prioritization may impose constraints on per-channel latency budgets. Risk: if Network Link cannot guarantee channel isolation (e.g., due to bandwidth contention, jitter, packet loss), the latency budgets defined in DP3 may become infeasible. Mitigation: establish Network Link SLA upfront (bandwidth guarantee, jitter bounds, loss rate); if SLA cannot be met, revise DP3 to include adaptive latency budgeting, fallback modes, or network-aware channel scheduling.

Deterministic pipeline (DP1) integration with Vehicle-side processing is a critical co-design dependency. Risk: if Vehicle module does not implement a matching deterministic pipeline for command execution and feedback generation, end-to-end latency guarantees fail despite correct Teleoperation channel architecture. Mitigation: DP1 and DP3 must be co-designed and verified together; Vehicle module specification must include deterministic pipeline requirements; establish end-to-end latency verification protocol spanning Teleoperation and Vehicle modules.

Cross-channel interference prevention (priority inversion, starvation, buffer overflow) is complex in real-time systems. Risk: if scheduler, queue, or timeout handler logic is not carefully abstracted, implementation details may leak into Network Link or Vehicle modules, violating IM boundaries. Mitigation: encapsulate all channel scheduling, buffering, and timeout enforcement within Teleoperation module; define clear internal sub-module boundaries (command channel handler, feedback channel handler, safety channel handler) with explicit queue and scheduler ownership.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score). DP3 is fundamentally a teleoperation-domain concern: channel decomposition, per-channel latency budgeting, and cross-channel isolation are core to remote operation architecture. The three functional channels and their isolation mechanisms naturally reside within the Teleoperation boundary. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined contracts that do not require diffusion of core DP3 logic. Minor diffusion risk is limited to well-understood dependencies: SafetyAlert requires coordination with Safety Services (policy-level), and Network Link substrate provides the communication medium. These are external contracts, not internal diffusion. Recommended: document the internal sub-module structure (command channel, feedback channel, safety channel handlers) to make the three-channel decomposition explicit within the Teleoperation IM boundary.

recommended_revisions: Establish explicit co-design protocol between DP1 (deterministic pipeline) and DP3 (channel decomposition). Create a joint specification document that defines: (1) end-to-end latency budget allocation across Teleoperation and Vehicle modules; (2) deterministic pipeline stages in both modules; (3) latency measurement points and verification methodology. This ensures DP1 and DP3 are not designed in isolation.

Define SafetyAlert interface contract with explicit schema, semantics, and versioning. Clarify which safety constraints are enforced at the Teleoperation channel level (e.g., timeout on safety alert response, priority queuing of safety commands) versus which are enforced at the Safety Services policy level (e.g., constraint validation, policy logic). Document this split to prevent diffusion and enable independent evolution of safety policy and teleoperation channel architecture.

Establish Network Link SLA upfront, including: (1) bandwidth guarantee per channel (or total); (2) jitter bounds; (3) packet loss rate; (4) latency bounds on the network substrate itself. If SLA cannot be met, revise DP3 to include adaptive latency budgeting, fallback modes, or network-aware channel scheduling. Document the SLA as a constraint on DP3 feasibility.

Decompose the Teleoperation IM boundary into three internal sub-modules (or logical components): (1) Command Channel Handler (processes Tasking input, enforces command latency budget, manages command queue and timeout); (2) Feedback Channel Handler (processes Telemetry output, enforces feedback latency budget, manages feedback buffer and jitter); (3) Safety Channel Handler (processes SafetyAlert bidirectional, enforces safety channel priority and isolation, manages safety queue). This makes the three-channel decomposition (DP3) explicit and verifiable within the IM boundary.

Create an end-to-end latency verification plan that spans Teleoperation and Vehicle modules. Define measurement points at: (1) Tasking input to Teleoperation; (2) Teleoperation output to Network Link; (3) Network Link output to Vehicle; (4) Vehicle command execution; (5) Vehicle feedback generation; (6) Network Link output to Teleoperation; (7) Teleoperation Telemetry output. Establish acceptance criteria for p95 latency at each stage and for end-to-end latency (≤ 150 ms). This enables verification of DP1 + DP3 integration.

Document the isolation mechanisms for cross-channel interference prevention within Teleoperation. Specify: (1) queue isolation (separate queues per channel or priority-based scheduling); (2) buffer isolation (dedicated buffers per channel); (3) timeout enforcement (per-channel timeout handlers); (4) priority inversion prevention (e.g., priority inheritance, priority ceiling). This makes the interference prevention aspect of DP3 concrete and testable.

decision.justification: Gate results show all three gates (FR-IM, FR-DP, DP-IM) passing with high scores (0.92 containment, High verification feasibility, High allocation clarity). However, the recommended_revisions and diffusion_risks reveal that DP3 (channel decomposition) introduces critical co-design dependencies and internal architectural complexity that must be decomposed into explicit sub-FRs before implementation. Specifically: (1) DP1-DP3 co-design dependency requires joint specification of end-to-end latency allocation and deterministic pipeline stages across Teleoperation and Vehicle modules—this is a sub-FR concern. (2) SafetyAlert interface contract and safety constraint split between channel-level and policy-level enforcement requires explicit functional decomposition. (3) Cross-channel interference prevention (priority inversion, starvation, buffer overflow) is complex enough to warrant dedicated sub-FRs for queue isolation, buffer isolation, and timeout enforcement. (4) Network Link SLA feasibility is a constraint that may trigger adaptive latency budgeting—a sub-FR decision point. The current level (FR: p95 latency ≤ 150 ms, DP3: channel decomposition) is architecturally sound but operationally incomplete. Decomposing into sub-FRs will make the internal structure of Teleoperation IM explicit, enable verification of channel isolation mechanisms, and surface co-design dependencies with Vehicle and Safety Services modules early. Safety criticality (high) reinforces the need for explicit sub-FR decomposition of safety channel handling and cross-channel interference prevention.
next_level_sub_frs: FR3.1: Enforce independent latency budgets for command, feedback, and safety functional channels such that each channel meets its allocated latency target (command ≤ 50 ms, feedback ≤ 60 ms, safety ≤ 40 ms) without interference from other channels.

FR3.2: Isolate command, feedback, and safety channels from cross-channel interference (priority inversion, queue starvation, buffer overflow) such that degradation in one channel does not degrade latency guarantees in other channels.

FR3.3: Enforce safety channel priority and timeout constraints such that safety alerts are processed with highest priority and timeout-expired safety commands trigger safe fallback behavior (e.g., vehicle halt, operator alert).

FR3.4: Coordinate with Safety Services module to enforce safety constraints at the SafetyAlert interface such that channel-level enforcement (timeout, priority, isolation) is decoupled from policy-level enforcement (constraint validation, policy logic).
FR3.5: Allocate end-to-end latency budget across Teleoperation and Vehicle modules such that deterministic pipeline stages in both modules are co-designed and verified to meet p95 latency ≤ 150 ms.
FR3.6: Validate Network Link substrate SLA (bandwidth, jitter, loss rate) against per-channel latency budgets and trigger adaptive latency budgeting or fallback modes if SLA cannot be guaranteed.

revise.why: DP3 (channel decomposition with independent latency budgets and isolation mechanisms) is architecturally correct but requires explicit decomposition of internal mechanisms (queue isolation, buffer isolation, timeout enforcement, priority scheduling) into sub-DPs. The recommended_revisions call for: (1) explicit internal sub-module structure (Command Channel Handler, Feedback Channel Handler, Safety Channel Handler); (2) SafetyAlert interface contract with explicit schema and versioning; (3) Network Link SLA upfront with adaptive budgeting fallback; (4) end-to-end latency verification plan spanning Teleoperation and Vehicle. These are DP-level details that should be surfaced at the next decomposition level. Revise DP3 to include a summary of internal sub-module responsibilities and interface contracts, then decompose into sub-DPs for each sub-FR.

assumptions: Network Link substrate can provide bandwidth guarantee, jitter bounds, and loss rate SLA sufficient to support per-channel latency budgets (command ≤ 50 ms, feedback ≤ 60 ms, safety ≤ 40 ms). If SLA cannot be met, adaptive latency budgeting or fallback modes will be required.

Vehicle module implements a matching deterministic pipeline for command execution and feedback generation, enabling end-to-end latency guarantees. Co-design protocol between Teleoperation and Vehicle modules will be established upfront.

Safety Services module owns policy-level safety constraint validation and logic; Teleoperation module enforces only channel-level constraints (priority, timeout, buffer isolation) independent of policy content. SafetyAlert interface will be versioned and schema-driven to enable independent evolution.

Cross-channel interference prevention (priority inversion, starvation, buffer overflow) can be achieved through standard real-time scheduling techniques (priority inheritance, priority ceiling, separate queues, dedicated buffers) without requiring novel algorithms.

Teleoperation module boundary is sufficient to contain all three functional channels and their isolation mechanisms; no diffusion of core channel decomposition logic to Network Link or Vehicle modules is required.
open_risks: DP1-DP3 co-design dependency: if Vehicle module does not implement deterministic pipeline matching Teleoperation's channel architecture, end-to-end latency guarantees fail. Mitigation: establish joint specification document and co-design protocol; include Vehicle module latency requirements in sub-FR3.5.

SafetyAlert interface coupling: if safety policy semantics change (new constraint types, alert formats), Teleoperation channel-level enforcement logic may require adaptation. Mitigation: establish SafetyAlert as versioned contract interface with explicit schema; clarify split between channel-level and policy-level enforcement in sub-FR3.4.

Network Link SLA feasibility: if Network Link cannot guarantee per-channel latency budgets due to bandwidth contention, jitter, or packet loss, latency budgets defined in DP3 become infeasible. Mitigation: establish Network Link SLA upfront; if SLA cannot be met, revise DP3 to include adaptive latency budgeting or fallback modes (sub-FR3.6).

Cross-channel interference complexity: if scheduler, queue, or timeout handler logic is not carefully abstracted, implementation details may leak into Network Link or Vehicle modules, violating IM boundaries. Mitigation: decompose Teleoperation IM into three internal sub-modules (Command Channel Handler, Feedback Channel Handler, Safety Channel Handler) with explicit ownership and interfaces (sub-FR3.1, 3.2, 3.3).

Safety channel timeout enforcement: if safety alert timeout is too short, operator may not have time to respond; if too long, vehicle may remain in unsafe state. Mitigation: establish safety timeout value through hazard analysis and operator studies; include in sub-FR3.3 acceptance criteria.

Verification complexity: end-to-end latency verification spanning Teleoperation and Vehicle modules under realistic network conditions (jitter, loss, contention) is complex. Mitigation: create detailed end-to-end latency verification plan with measurement points at each stage (sub-FR3.5); establish acceptance criteria for p95 latency at each stage and end-to-end.
evidence_needed: Network Link SLA specification: bandwidth guarantee per channel (or total), jitter bounds, packet loss rate, latency bounds on network substrate itself. Required to validate feasibility of per-channel latency budgets (sub-FR3.1, 3.6).

Vehicle module deterministic pipeline specification: pipeline stages, latency per stage, command execution and feedback generation timing. Required to validate end-to-end latency allocation (sub-FR3.5).
SafetyAlert interface contract: explicit schema, semantics, versioning, and split between channel-level and policy-level enforcement. Required to validate safety constraint coordination (sub-FR3.4).

Cross-channel interference prevention design: queue isolation strategy (separate queues vs. priority-based scheduling), buffer isolation (dedicated buffers per channel), timeout enforcement (per-channel timeout handlers), priority inversion prevention (priority inheritance, priority ceiling). Required to validate sub-FRs 3.2 and 3.3.

End-to-end latency verification plan: measurement points at Tasking input, Teleoperation output, Network Link output, Vehicle command execution, Vehicle feedback generation, Network Link output to Teleoperation, Telemetry output. Acceptance criteria for p95 latency at each stage and end-to-end (≤ 150 ms). Required to validate sub-FR3.5.

Safety timeout value and operator response time study: hazard analysis identifying safe timeout bounds, operator studies measuring typical response time to safety alerts. Required to validate sub-FR3.3 acceptance criteria.

Teleoperation internal sub-module design: Command Channel Handler, Feedback Channel Handler, Safety Channel Handler responsibilities, interfaces, and ownership. Required to validate sub-FRs 3.1, 3.2, 3.3 and make DP3 decomposition explicit.
